subject: Criminal Law
module_title: Public Order, Offences Against Justice, and State Offences

questions.question: Article 39 of the Irish Constitution defines treason. What is the constitutional definition of treason, and what is the maximum sentence under s.2 of the Criminal Justice Act 1990?

options.A: Treason is defined in ordinary legislation, not the Constitution — the maximum sentence is life imprisonment
options.B: Article 39 of the Constitution defines treason as levying war against the State or assisting any state or person or inciting or conspiring with any persons to levy war against the State, or attempting by force of arms or other violent means to overthrow the organs of government established by the Constitution, or taking part or being concerned in or inciting or conspiring with any persons to make or to take part or be concerned in any such attempt; the maximum sentence under s.2 of the Criminal Justice Act 1990 is 40 years imprisonment
options.C: Article 39 defines treason solely as assisting a foreign power to invade Ireland — acts of internal subversion are not treason
options.D: Article 39 defines treason as any act that undermines public confidence in the government — the maximum sentence is 20 years

questions.explanation: Treason in Ireland has constitutional status — Article 39 of the Constitution itself defines the offence, which is unusual as most criminal offences are created by statute. Article 39 defines treason as: levying war against the State; assisting any state or person in levying war against Ireland; attempting by force of arms or violent means to overthrow the organs of government established by the Constitution; or taking part in, inciting, or conspiring to make any such attempt. The maximum sentence for treason is 40 years imprisonment under s.2 of the Criminal Justice Act 1990. The 40-year maximum reflects the unique gravity of treason — an offence against the very foundations of the State. FE-1 candidates must know the constitutional basis (Art 39) and the statutory maximum (40 years, CJA 1990). Option A incorrectly denies the constitutional definition. Option C incorrectly narrows treason to foreign invasion. Option D gives an incorrect definition and maximum.

questions.question: Section 21(1) of the Offences Against the State Act 1939 creates the offence of membership of an unlawful organisation. What is the maximum sentence, and what evidential provision accompanies this offence?

options.A: The maximum sentence is 3 years; there is no special evidential provision
options.B: The maximum sentence under s.21(1) OATSA 1939 is 7 years imprisonment; s.21(3) provides that a person who is found to be a member of an unlawful organisation at a time when the organisation was a scheduled organisation — or who refuses to answer questions or gives false information — is presumed guilty; this reverse onus provision shifts the burden of proof to the accused to rebut the presumption of membership
options.C: The maximum sentence is 10 years; the prosecution must prove membership beyond reasonable doubt with no special evidential provisions
options.D: The maximum sentence is life imprisonment, reflecting the existential threat to the State posed by unlawful organisations

questions.explanation: Section 21(1) of the Offences Against the State Act 1939 creates the offence of membership of an unlawful organisation (including proscribed organisations such as the IRA). The maximum sentence is 7 years imprisonment. Section 21(3) OATSA 1939 provides a significant evidential provision: a person is presumed to be a member of an unlawful organisation if a Chief Superintendent believes them to be a member and gives evidence to that effect, or if they are found to be a member by a court in prior proceedings. This is a reverse onus provision — the accused must rebut the presumption on the balance of probabilities. This provision has been the subject of constitutional challenges regarding the presumption of innocence under Art 38.1 — the courts have generally upheld it as a proportionate response to the operational difficulties of proving unlawful organisation membership. Option A states an incorrect maximum and denies the evidential provision. Option C states an incorrect maximum and denies the evidential provision. Option D states an incorrect maximum.

questions.question: Section 6 of the Criminal Law Act 1976 creates the offence of recruiting persons for unlawful organisations. What is the maximum sentence?

options.B: 7 years imprisonment on indictment — the same as membership under s.21(1) OATSA 1939
options.D: Life imprisonment — recruiting is treated as equivalent in gravity to directing an unlawful organisation

questions.explanation: Section 3 of the Criminal Law Act 1976 (note: sometimes referenced as s.6 in different editions — check the specific provision carefully) creates the offence of recruiting persons for unlawful organisations. The maximum sentence for this offence is 10 years imprisonment on indictment. This is higher than the 7-year maximum for membership itself under s.21(1) OATSA 1939, reflecting the greater harm caused by those who grow the membership of dangerous organisations as compared to members themselves. The graduated sentencing framework under the offences against the State regime reflects different levels of culpability: membership (7 years); recruiting (10 years); directing — life imprisonment (s.6 Criminal Law Act 1998). Option A states an incorrect lower maximum. Option B equates the sentence with membership — incorrect. Option D equates the sentence with directing — incorrect.

questions.question: Section 6 of the Criminal Law Act 1998 creates the most serious offence in the offences against the State regime. What is this offence, and what is the maximum sentence?

options.A: Section 6 CLA 1998 creates the offence of membership of an unlawful organisation — maximum 7 years
options.B: Section 6 CLA 1998 creates the offence of directing the activities of an unlawful organisation — the maximum sentence is life imprisonment, reflecting the legislature's view that those who direct and control dangerous unlawful organisations at the highest level pose the greatest threat and deserve the most severe sanction
options.C: Section 6 CLA 1998 creates the offence of financing terrorist activities — maximum 20 years
options.D: Section 6 CLA 1998 creates the offence of conspiracy to overthrow the State — maximum 40 years (the same as treason)

questions.explanation: Section 6 of the Criminal Law Act 1998 creates the offence of directing the activities of an unlawful organisation. The maximum sentence is life imprisonment — making this the most serious offence in the offences against the State regime, exceeding even the 40-year maximum for treason (in terms of the possible sentence range). The rationale is that those who direct and control dangerous organisations at the highest level — providing strategic leadership and operational direction — bear the greatest responsibility for the harm caused by those organisations and deserve the most severe sanction available. The graduated framework: membership s.21(1) OATSA (7 years); recruiting CLA 1976 (10 years); directing s.6 CLA 1998 (life). FE-1 candidates must know this hierarchy. Options A, C, and D all misidentify the offence or the maximum sentence.

questions.question: Section 6 of the Offences Against the State Act 1939 creates the offence of usurpation of the functions of government. What is the maximum sentence?

options.B: 20 years imprisonment on indictment — reflecting the grave constitutional threat posed by usurpation of government functions
options.C: Life imprisonment — the same as directing an unlawful organisation
options.D: 40 years imprisonment — the same as treason, which usurpation closely resembles

questions.explanation: Section 6 of the Offences Against the State Act 1939 creates the offence of usurping or attempting to usurp the functions of the government. The maximum sentence is 20 years imprisonment on indictment. Usurpation — attempting to seize or exercise the functions of government unlawfully — is a grave constitutional offence though it is punished less severely than treason (40 years) or directing an unlawful organisation (life). The 20-year maximum reflects the serious threat to constitutional order posed by usurpation while maintaining the proportionality principle in the graduated sentencing framework for offences against the State. Option A states an incorrect lower maximum. Option C equates it with directing — incorrect. Option D equates it with treason — incorrect.

questions.question: In DPP v Kehoe [2012] (Ireland), the Special Criminal Court convicted the accused of membership of an unlawful organisation. What does this case illustrate about the operation of the offences against the State regime?

options.A: The case established that membership of an unlawful organisation can only be established through direct evidence of participation in specific criminal acts
options.B: DPP v Kehoe [2012] illustrates how the Special Criminal Court applies the OATSA 1939 membership offence in practice, including the use of Garda Chief Superintendent belief evidence under s.21(3) to establish the presumption of membership; the case demonstrates the Special Criminal Court's role as the tribunal for serious offences against the State where the ordinary courts are inadequate to secure the effective administration of justice
options.C: The case established that the reverse onus provision in s.21(3) OATSA 1939 is unconstitutional and was struck down
options.D: The case established that the accused must be shown to have taken an active part in recent operations of the unlawful organisation — passive or historical membership is insufficient

questions.explanation: DPP v Kehoe [2012] is an important illustration of the operation of the offences against the State regime in practice. The Special Criminal Court — the three-judge, non-jury tribunal established under Art 38.3.1 of the Constitution for offences against the State and related matters — convicted the accused of membership of an unlawful organisation. The case demonstrates: (1) the use of Chief Superintendent belief evidence under s.21(3) OATSA 1939 to establish the presumption of membership; (2) the operation of the Special Criminal Court's procedures and jurisdiction; and (3) how the courts approach proof of membership, which often involves circumstantial and inference-based evidence given the clandestine nature of unlawful organisations. Option A incorrectly requires direct evidence of specific criminal acts. Option C incorrectly states s.21(3) was struck down — it was not. Option D incorrectly imposes an 'active recent operations' requirement.

questions.question: Paul is found attending meetings of an organisation that has been declared an unlawful organisation under the OATSA 1939. A Chief Superintendent gives evidence that he believes Paul to be a member of the organisation. Paul gives no evidence. What is the likely outcome under the OATSA 1939 framework?

options.A: Paul cannot be convicted on the basis of the Chief Superintendent's belief evidence alone — direct evidence of participation in the organisation's activities is required
options.B: Under s.21(3) OATSA 1939, the Chief Superintendent's belief evidence triggers a presumption of membership; as Paul has given no evidence to rebut this presumption, the court is entitled to find the presumption unrebutted and convict Paul of membership of an unlawful organisation under s.21(1); the maximum sentence is 7 years; attendance at meetings and the Chief Superintendent's belief evidence combine to establish the prosecution's case
options.C: Paul can only be convicted if the prosecution also proves that he knew the organisation was unlawful — lack of knowledge is a full defence
options.D: The Chief Superintendent's belief evidence is inadmissible — only documentary evidence of membership (such as a membership card or written records) is admissible to prove membership

questions.explanation: This scenario tests the operation of the s.21(3) OATSA 1939 evidential provision. The analysis: (1) The organisation is a declared unlawful organisation — this is a prerequisite for the s.21(1) offence; (2) A Chief Superintendent has given evidence of belief that Paul is a member — this triggers the statutory presumption under s.21(3); (3) Paul has given no evidence to rebut the presumption — the burden shifts to the accused under s.21(3) and he has not discharged it; (4) Attendance at meetings is additional circumstantial evidence; (5) The court is entitled to convict under s.21(1) — maximum 7 years. The case would likely be tried in the Special Criminal Court given the nature of the offence. Option A incorrectly requires direct participation evidence. Option C incorrectly introduces a knowledge requirement — the presumption operates without requiring proof of knowledge of unlawfulness. Option D incorrectly characterises Chief Superintendent evidence as inadmissible.

questions.question: The Special Criminal Court was established under Article 38.3.1 of the Constitution. What is the basis for the court's jurisdiction and its composition?

options.A: The Special Criminal Court is established by ordinary statute — it has no constitutional basis and can be abolished at any time by a simple Act of the Oireachtas
options.B: The Special Criminal Court is established under Article 38.3.1 of the Constitution, which permits the establishment of special courts for the trial of offences in cases where the ordinary courts are inadequate to secure the effective administration of justice and the preservation of public peace and order; the court is composed of three judges (typically a judge from each of the High Court, Circuit Court, and District Court); it sits without a jury; its jurisdiction is activated by Part V of the Offences Against the State Act 1939
options.C: The Special Criminal Court is composed of five judges and may only try offences under the OATSA 1939 — it has no jurisdiction over any other criminal offences
options.D: The Special Criminal Court was established by emergency legislation in 1996 and has no permanent constitutional basis — it must be renewed periodically by the Oireachtas

questions.explanation: The Special Criminal Court has constitutional status under Article 38.3.1, which provides that special courts may be established by law for the trial of offences 'in cases where it may be determined in accordance with such law that the ordinary courts are inadequate to secure the effective administration of justice, and the preservation of public peace and order.' The court's practical operation is governed by Part V of the Offences Against the State Act 1939. Key features: (1) No jury — the court sits with three judges; (2) Three judges — traditionally drawn from the High Court, Circuit Court, and District Court; (3) Broad jurisdiction — it can try serious offences against the State and has been expanded to other serious offences. Kavanagh v Ireland [1996] and DPP v Dowdall and Hutch [2022] are significant cases on the Special Criminal Court's jurisdiction. Option A incorrectly denies the constitutional basis. Option C incorrectly states five judges and limits jurisdiction to OATSA offences. Option D incorrectly claims emergency legislation as the basis.
